The Great Sphinx of Giza is predominantly composed of **limestone**, which was carved directly from the natural bedrock of the Giza Plateau.[1](https://www.thegreatsphinxofgiza.com/faqs-about-the-great-sphinx), [2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Great_Sphinx_of_Giza), [3](https://www.ancient-egypt-online.com/the-great-sphinx.html), [4](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Great-Sphinx) This limestone is part of the Eocene-aged Mokattam Formation.[5](https://aeraweb.org/geology-of-the-sphinx/), [2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Great_Sphinx_of_Giza)

While limestone is the primary material, the rock layers forming the Sphinx also contain other minerals and components that influence its durability and weathering. These include water-soluble salts like halite (sodium chloride) and gypsum (calcium sulfate), as well as clastic components such as silt and clay minerals.[6](https://www.hallofmaat.com/sphinx/geologic-study-of-the-sphinx/), [7](https://www.hallofmaat.com/sphinx/deterioration-of-the-stone-of-the-great-sphinx/) The varying proportions of these components contribute to the different hardness levels observed in the Sphinx's body and head.[5](https://aeraweb.org/geology-of-the-sphinx/), [2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Great_Sphinx_of_Giza), [3](https://www.ancient-egypt-online.com/the-great-sphinx.html) The main mineral component of limestone is calcite.[8](https://geoexpro.com/the-great-sphinx-of-egypt-natures-shabby-chic-trick/)
